mission.title: Install and Configure Harbor on Kubernetes
mission.description: Harbor is an open-source trusted cloud-native registry project that stores, signs, and scans content. Installing Harbor allows you to manage your container images securely and efficiently with advanced features such as user management and access control.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Harbor Helm chart repository to your local Helm client using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add harbor https://helm.goharbor.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Harbor using Helm
steps.description:
Install Harbor with the specified version using Helm. Make sure to create a namespace for Harbor:
```bash
helm install harbor harbor/harbor --version v2.14.2 --namespace harbor --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if all the Harbor pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace harbor
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the values of the Harbor deployment to set resource limits. You can do this by creating a custom values file (harbor-values.yaml) and applying it:
```yaml
# harbor-values.yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "1"
    memory: "1Gi"
```
Then apply the configuration:
```bash
helm upgrade harbor harbor/harbor --version v2.14.2 --namespace harbor -f harbor-values.yaml
```

steps.title: Access Harbor UI
steps.description:
Port-forward the Harbor service to access the UI:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/harbor-harbor-portal 8080:80 --namespace harbor
```
You can now access the Harbor UI at http://localhost:8080.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Harbor will have all pods running in the 'harbor' namespace, and you will be able to access the Harbor UI at http://localhost:8080. Additionally, resource limits will be configured as specified.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add harbor https://helm.goharbor.io
helm repo update
helm install harbor harbor/harbor --version v2.14.2 --namespace harbor --create-namespace

# harbor-values.yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "1"
    memory: "1Gi"
helm upgrade harbor harbor/harbor --version v2.14.2 --namespace harbor -f harbor-values.yaml

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Harbor release using Helm to remove all associated resources from the cluster.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace harbor
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep harbor | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Harbor namespace and verify that all resources have been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace harbor
kubectl get all --namespace harbor
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current configuration and data. You can export the current Helm release configuration with:
```bash
helm get values harbor --namespace harbor > harbor-backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Harbor release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade harbor harbor/harbor --version v2.14.3 --namespace harbor -f harbor-backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if all the Harbor pods are running successfully after the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace harbor
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue may occur due to misconfigurations or resource limits. To diagnose, check the logs of the affected pod:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace harbor
```Fix any configuration issues and redeploy the pod.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to access Harbor UI
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Harbor UI, ensure that the port-forward command is running. Check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace harbor
```If the service is not running, restart the Harbor deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during an upgrade, ensure that the version you are trying to upgrade to is compatible with the current version. You can check the current version with:
```bash
helm list --namespace harbor
```Rollback to the previous version if necessary:
```bash
helm rollback harbor <revision-number> --namespace harbor
```

troubleshooting.title: Persistent volume claims not released
troubleshooting.description:
If PVCs are not being released after uninstalling, check the reclaim policy of the storage class. To view the PVCs, run:
```bash
kubectl get pvc --namespace harbor
```If needed, manually delete the PVCs with:
```bash
kubectl delete pvc <pvc-name> --namespace harbor
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and Helm installed to deploy Harbor.
